description: Figure 1 comprises schematic diagrams of two experimental setups for cyclic testing of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels. Panel (a) depicts the thermocycling test apparatus for specimens with a 5 mm diameter working section. The specimen is mounted between restriction supports that impose an 80% thermal expansion restriction. Resistive heating up to 873 K is delivered through an AC circuit containing an automatic temperature-controlled variable resistor, while a contact thermocouple monitors temperature; cooling to 313 K is achieved via a jet of compressed air, enabling cyclic thermoplastic deformation. Panel (b) illustrates the ZDM-200PU elasto-plastic bending test machine configured for specimens with a 50 mm diameter working part. The specimen is clamped between a handle and fixed head, supported by a moving table, and subjected to a load Q, with the free-end deflection held constant at 150 mm. These complementary setups facilitate the investigation of thermal fatigue and elasto-plastic bending behavior, providing the experimental foundation for the conclusion that Fe-Mn-Al-C components can endure cyclic elasto-plastic loads combined with periodic temperature variations.

This figure presents S-N fatigue curves (stress amplitude versus number of cycles to failure) for austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C steel under three distinct test conditions: isothermal fatigue at 293 K (curve 1, black diamonds), isothermal fatigue at 673 K (curve 2, open circles), and thermocycling fatigue alternating between 293 K and 673 K (curve 3, open triangles). The vertical axis shows alternating stress σ in MPa, while the horizontal axis gives cycle count N in units of 10⁶. Curve 2 consistently shows the highest fatigue strength across the tested cycle range, with values near 500 MPa at low cycles decreasing to approximately 440 MPa at 10⁶ cycles. Curve 1 displays the lowest endurance, dropping from about 420 MPa to below 300 MPa over the same range. The thermocycling curve 3 occupies an intermediate position between these isothermal bounds. Several data points at higher cycles are marked with horizontal arrows indicating run-out specimens that did not fail. These results support the conclusion that the fatigue strength of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels at elevated temperature exceeds room-temperature performance, and that thermocycling behavior reflects time-dependent strengthening mechanisms during high-temperature exposure.

Figure 3 presents four optical micrographs comparing the microstructural evolution of austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C steel and martensitic Cr-Ni steel before and after cyclic testing, all captured at the same magnification with a 30 μm scale bar. Subfigures (a) and (b) show the austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C steel in the initial condition and after tests, respectively; the caption states that thermal cycling causes thickening of grain-boundary κ-phase precipitates during high-temperature aging. Subfigures (c) and (d) display the martensitic Cr-Ni steel before and after testing; citation sentences report that the original martensitic structure decomposes into a ferrite-carbide equilibrium mixture, the oriented martensitic morphology disappears, and mechanical strength decreases after cycling. The micrographs reveal that the Fe-Mn-Al-C alloy retains a stable austenitic matrix with only grain-boundary precipitation, whereas the martensitic steel undergoes extensive phase decomposition and loss of orientation. This contrast supports the core conclusion that Fe-Mn-Al-C steels possess superior microstructural stability and cyclic endurance relative to martensitic Cr-Ni steels under combined elasto-plastic and thermal cyclic loading.

Figure 4 presents force-deflection hysteresis loops for an austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C steel specimen (50 mm diameter, water-quenched from 1323 K) subjected to elasto-plastic cyclic bending. The graph plots applied load Q (×10³ N) versus deflection H (mm), showing three distinct loops corresponding to the 1st cycle (solid line), 2nd cycle (dashed line), and the last (12th) cycle (dash-dotted line). The first cycle reaches approximately H = 150 mm at Q×10³ ≈ 30 N, while the 12th cycle shows reduced maximum deflection near H = 100 mm at Q×10³ ≈ 35 N. The hysteresis loops remain nearly stable in shape and enclosed area across cycles, indicating that the work expended on elasto-plastic deformation per cycle practically does not vary. This stable energy dissipation behavior supports the conclusion that Fe-Mn-Al-C steels can endure elasto-plastic cyclic loading with high fatigue resistance, sustaining four times more cycles than comparable martensitic chromium steels under similar conditions.

This figure presents the true stress versus true strain curve for Fe-Mn-Al-C austenite, illustrating its strain hardening behavior under a strain rate of 1 mm/min (as stated in the caption). The curve demonstrates a substantial capacity for work hardening, with true stress rising steeply from the origin to approximately 800 MPa at a true strain of 0.1, and continuing to increase monotonically to about 1400 MPa at a true strain near 0.37. The plot reveals an initially high hardening rate that transitions into a sustained, nearly linear hardening regime at larger strains. The accompanying text indicates that this exceptional strengthening is attributed to the austenitic structure's deformation mechanism, primarily mechanical twinning, where deformation twins act as barriers to dislocation glide and further twinning. With increasing deformation, the dislocation density increases sharply, forming localized tangles at intersections of slip systems. These microstructural evolution features underpin the alloy's high endurance under elasto-plastic cyclic loading and the invariance of absorbed energy per cycle.

This figure presents two transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs illustrating the dislocation structure developed in Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel after cold working. Subfigure (a) shows the microstructure after 10% cold work, whereas subfigure (b) displays the structure after 20% cold work, both captured with a visible scale bar of 625 nm. Progressing from 10% to 20% deformation, the images reveal a marked increase in dislocation density and the formation of localized dislocation tangles at intersections of slip systems. These micrographs provide direct visual evidence for the strain-hardening mechanism described in the text, where deformation twins and accumulated dislocations act as obstacles to further plastic deformation. The progressive creation of such highly strengthened local volumes during cyclic loading, as reflected in these static post-deformation images, underpins the alloy's high endurance under elasto-plastic deformation and the nearly constant absorbed energy per cycle. Consequently, this figure supports the core conclusion that dislocation substructure evolution governs the superior fatigue performance of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels.
